question_title: Will the IAEA report that the Zaporizhzhya NPP has regained a second operational off-site power line before June 14, 2026?
summary: The ZNPP's 750 kV Dniprovska power line has been disconnected since March 24, 2026 (72+ days), leaving the plant reliant on a single 330 kV backup line. For YES resolution, IAEA must confirm two lines connected simultaneously by June 13. This requires a ceasefire agreement (not achieved despite 72 days of negotiations), followed by demining and repairs (historically 6-10 days minimum), followed by IAEA publication — all within 9 remaining days. The military situation is actively escalating (drone strikes June 1 and 3, 17th blackout, communications disruptions), making a ceasefire agreement less likely, not more. IAEA Updates 351 and 352 show no progress language. While the IAEA has brokered 5 successful ceasefires previously and the June 5 Board meeting adds pressure, the tight timeline and hostile military conditions make restoration within the window very unlikely. The 5% probability accounts for genuine surprise diplomatic breakthroughs.

factors.description: Dniprovska 750 kV line has been disconnected for 72+ days (since Mar 24) with no ceasefire agreement reached despite ongoing IAEA negotiations. The longer it remains down without agreement, the harder conditions clearly are.

Active military escalation in late May/early June 2026: drone strike on ZNPP turbine building Jun 1, drone strike on Nikopol substation causing 17th total blackout Jun 3, communications blackout May 27, 160+ UAVs near nuclear sites. This environment is hostile to ceasefire agreements.

Restoration requires a multi-step process: ceasefire negotiation → demining → physical repairs → reconnection → IAEA confirmation in published update. Historical minimum ~6-7 days even after ceasefire agreed, vs only 9 days left in resolution window.

IAEA Update 351 (May 22) and Update 352 (May 28) both confirm ZNPP 'remains reliant on its last off-site power line' with only vague language about continuing negotiations — no sign of imminent breakthrough.

IAEA Board meeting June 5 could increase political pressure for action, and IAEA has successfully brokered 5 previous ceasefires. Grossi is actively pushing for restoration. The 17th blackout (Jun 3) increases urgency.

Historical precedent shows restorations ranged 23-187 days. In fastest case (23 days), ceasefire was agreed first. Current 72-day disconnection without ceasefire is already the longest such period without agreement.

reasoning:
**My forecast: 5% probability (YES)**

**Key reasoning:**

The ZNPP currently operates on a single 330 kV backup line. For this question to resolve YES, the IAEA must confirm two simultaneous power lines by June 13, 2026. This requires:

1. **A ceasefire agreement** — After 72 days of failed negotiations and military escalation (drone strikes on June 1 and 3, 17th total blackout), there's no sign of an imminent breakthrough
2. **Demining + repairs** — Historically takes 6-10 days minimum after ceasefire
3. **IAEA confirmation** in a published update — Requires publication within the window

With only 9 days remaining and the military situation actively deteriorating (not improving), the entire chain completing in time is very unlikely. The 5% accounts for surprise scenarios like an unexpected diplomatic breakthrough at the June 5 IAEA Board meeting or undisclosed behind-the-scenes progress.
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) Will the IAEA report that the Zaporizhzhya NPP has regained a second operational off-site power line before June 14, 2026?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/43770)
[Drone Strike Cuts Last External Power Line to Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Plant Temporarily, IAEA Says](https://www.kyivpost.com/post/77425)
[Occupied Zaporizhzhia NPP Suffers Another Blackout, IAEA Says](https://biz.liga.net/en/energy/news/occupied-zaporizhzhia-npp-suffers-another-blackout-iaea-says)
resolution_criteria:
This question resolves YES if any IAEA Director General Update on the situation in Ukraine published between May 31, 2026 and June 13, 2026 (inclusive) states that the Zaporizhzhya Nuclear Power Plant (ZNPP) is connected to, or receiving off-site power from, two or more external (off-site) power lines simultaneously. The relevant updates are published on the IAEA's "Nuclear Safety, Security and Safeguards in Ukraine — DG Updates" page (https://www.iaea.org/topics/response/nuclear-safety-security-and-safeguards-in-ukraine/dg-updates). Typical YES language would be phrasing such as "ZNPP is now connected to two off-site power lines," confirmation that the 750 kV Dniprovska line has been re-energised in addition to the 330 kV Ferosplavna-1 line, or equivalent.

The question resolves NO if all IAEA updates in this window continue to describe ZNPP as relying on a single ("last," "sole," "only remaining") off-site power line, or if no IAEA update during the window addresses the off-site power configuration. If the IAEA does not publish any update during the window, the most recent IAEA Update available as of 23:59 UTC on June 13, 2026 will be used, and the question resolves NO unless that update describes two or more off-site lines being available.
fine_print: A line counts as "operational" / "available" if the IAEA describes it as connected, restored, re-energised, or supplying power to the plant, even if it is described as fragile or as a backup. Brief disconnections lasting under 24 hours that the IAEA does not treat as a reversion to single-line status do not block a YES resolution if a subsequent IAEA update in the window describes two lines as being available. Reporting by Ukrainian, Russian, or other media sources alone (without IAEA confirmation in a DG Update) does not resolve the question. If the IAEA's DG Updates page is unavailable, the corresponding IAEA press release pages or [credible sources](https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) summarising the relevant IAEA statement may be used.

factors.description: Dniprovska 750 kV line has been disconnected for 72+ days (since Mar 24) with no ceasefire agreement reached despite ongoing IAEA negotiations. The longer it remains down, the harder conditions clearly are.

Active military escalation in late May/early June: drone strike on turbine building Jun 1, drone strike causing 17th blackout Jun 3, communications blackout May 27, 160+ UAVs near nuclear sites. This environment is hostile to ceasefire agreements.

Restoration requires a multi-step process: ceasefire negotiation → demining → physical repairs → reconnection → IAEA confirmation in published update. Minimum ~7-10 days even if ceasefire agreed immediately, vs 9 days left in window.
IAEA Update 351 (May 22) and 352 (May 28) both confirm single-line status with no sign of imminent ceasefire breakthrough. No progress language in updates.
IAEA Board meeting June 5 could increase political pressure for action, and IAEA has successfully brokered 5 previous ceasefires. Grossi is actively pushing for this.

Historical precedent: past restorations took 23-187 days. In 2 of 3 cases, restoration happened within the current elapsed time (72 days), but the current case shows no ceasefire agreement even being close.

input.anchor: Base rate: Given that the line has been down 72 days with no ceasefire agreed, and only 9 days remain, the conditional probability of restoration in any given 9-day window during active conflict without a ceasefire agreement is very low. Historical pattern shows restorations require ~1-4 weeks AFTER ceasefire agreement, and no ceasefire is in sight. ~5% base.
input.assessment:
The question asks whether IAEA will confirm ZNPP has two or more off-site power lines connected simultaneously before June 14, 2026. Currently only the 330 kV Ferosplavna-1 line is operational; the 750 kV Dniprovska line has been disconnected since March 24, 2026.

For YES to happen, a complex chain of events must occur within 9 days:
1. IAEA must successfully broker a ceasefire (ongoing negotiations for 72+ days with no breakthrough)
2. Demining and repair work must be completed (historically takes several days to a week)
3. The line must be reconnected
4. IAEA must publish an update confirming two-line status

Against this timeline, the military situation has been ESCALATING, not improving:
- June 1: Drone strike on ZNPP turbine building
- June 3: 17th total blackout from drone strike on Nikopol substation
- May 27: Communications blackout at site
- 160+ UAVs recorded near nuclear sites in a 24-hour period

The IAEA Board meeting on June 5 could theoretically catalyze action, but Board meetings have not historically produced immediate on-the-ground ceasefires. The pattern shows weeks of negotiation precede each ceasefire.

Even in the most optimistic scenario (ceasefire agreed June 5, immediate start of repairs), completing repairs and getting IAEA confirmation by June 13 would be extremely tight.

The probability is very low - approximately 5%.
input.calibration_notes: Status quo strongly favors NO. The Dniprovska line remains disconnected and military activity is intensifying. Even if I'm underestimating the probability of a surprise ceasefire, the tight timeline for repairs + IAEA confirmation makes YES very difficult. My 5% accounts for genuine surprise scenarios (unexpected diplomatic breakthrough, rapid repair if conditions suddenly permit).
input.key_uncertainties: 1. Whether the IAEA Board meeting June 5 could produce an unexpected diplomatic push that rapidly leads to ceasefire. 2. Whether there are behind-the-scenes negotiations we don't know about that could produce a surprise agreement. 3. Whether repairs could be faster than historical precedent if conditions align.
input.tool_audit: Research tool: Excellent - provided comprehensive timeline of ZNPP power events. Web search: Very useful for finding IAEA update details and recent news. Fetch URL: Mixed - IAEA.org returned 403, but GlobalSecurity.org mirror worked well for Update 351 content. Code execution: Useful for organizing analysis.
input.update_triggers: A ceasefire announcement by IAEA would shift probability dramatically upward (to ~40-50%). News of successful completion of repairs would move it to ~85%+. Conversely, another blackout or military escalation would push toward 2-3%.
input.process_reflection: The research sub-agent was very effective at gathering comprehensive historical data. The main friction was IAEA.org blocking direct access (403), requiring workarounds via GlobalSecurity.org mirrors. The question was well-structured and the resolution criteria were clear, making analysis straightforward. The conditional chain decomposition (ceasefire × repairs × confirmation) was a natural fit for this question.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
**Checked all 6 factors against the research trace. No critical errors found.**

**Factor 1 (Dniprovska disconnected 72+ days since Mar 24, logit=-1.5):** Verified. IAEA Update 345 (Mar 26, 2026) via GlobalSecurity mirror states "ZNPP lost connection to its main off-site power line on Tuesday [24 March]." The worldview research entry confirms 72 days as of June 4, 2026. Grounded. ✓

**Factor 2 (Active military escalation — drone strikes Jun 1 and Jun 3, logit=-1.5):** Verified. Kyivpost article describes Jun 3 17th blackout from drone strike on Nikopol substation. Liga.net article independently confirms. Research entry mentions Jun 1 drone strike on turbine building. The "160+ UAVs" figure comes from IAEA Update 350 via the research sub-agent. Grounded. ✓

**Factor 3 (Multi-step process requiring 7-10 days minimum, logit=-1.0):** Verified. Historical data in trace shows Feb 27 ceasefire → Mar 5 reconnection = 6 days, and Oct 2025 ceasefire → ~1 week restoration. The 7-10 day estimate for the full chain (ceasefire → demining → repairs → reconnection → IAEA confirmation) is well-calibrated against these precedents. Grounded. ✓

**Factor 4 (Updates 351 and 352 confirm single-line status, logit=-1.0):** Verified. Update 351 content fetched from GlobalSecurity mirror: "ZNPP remains reliant on its last off-site power line, as the IAEA continues negotiations with both Ukraine and the Russian Federation." Update 352 content couldn't be directly fetched (403), but the Metaculus question background text states: "As of IAEA Updates 351 (22 May 2026) and 352 (28 May 2026), the plant still 'remains reliant on its last off-site power line.'" Grounded via the question text itself. ✓

**Factor 5 (IAEA Board meeting June 5 could catalyze action, logit=+0.5):** Verified. Research entry confirms "June 5, 2026: IAEA Board meeting scheduled." Web search results confirm Ukraine's MFA statement ahead of the Board meeting. The claim about 5 previous ceasefires is confirmed by the research entry. The modest positive logit (+0.5) with low confidence (0.5) is appropriately sized. ✓

**Factor 6 (Historical restoration times 23-187 days, logit=-0.5):** Verified. Trace confirms: 30 days (Sep-Oct 2025 Dniprovska), 187 days (May-Nov 2025 Ferosplavna-1), 23 days (Feb-Mar 2026 Ferosplavna-1). The "2 of 3 cases within 72 days" claim is correct (30 and 23 are both < 72). ✓

**No double-counting issues:** Factors 1, 3, and 4 share some correlation (all relate to the lack of ceasefire progress), but they capture distinct dimensions: Factor 1 is the duration of disconnection, Factor 3 is the process timeline, Factor 4 is the IAEA's current reporting status. The agent explicitly noted the factor sum implies ~1% but set the estimate at 5% to account for correlated factors, which is the correct direction of adjustment.

**No wrong-direction factors:** All factor signs are consistent with their descriptions. The only positive factor (5) correctly points toward YES.

**Resolution criteria alignment:** The agent directly engages with the specific resolution criteria — IAEA DG Updates between May 31 and June 13, two or more off-site power lines simultaneously. The analysis correctly identifies what "two lines" means (Dniprovska restored in addition to Ferosplavna-1) and the publication timeline. ✓

**Counterargument quality:** Strong. Constructs a coherent alternative story: IAEA Board meeting as catalyst, historical pattern of sudden ceasefires during crises, behind-the-scenes negotiations, and quantified "what would change my mind" triggers. Not a token gesture. ✓

**Confidence assessment (0.70):** Appropriate. Evidence strongly favors NO, but there is inherent unpredictability in diplomatic breakthroughs. ✓

**Probability assessment:** The 5% estimate is well-supported. The decomposition (P(ceasefire in ~5 days) ≈ 7.5% × P(repairs in time) ≈ 70% × P(IAEA confirms) ≈ 90% ≈ 4.7%) is coherent. My independent estimate based on the evidence would be 3-6%, so 5% is squarely within range. The agent correctly identifies that no ceasefire has been achieved despite 72 days of negotiation, military activity is escalating (not de-escalating), and the timeline is extremely tight.

**Cross-question consistency:** The related discrete forecast (Q43756, cumulative power losses) implicitly assumes ZNPP stays on a single line, with a 42% probability of additional power loss events. This is consistent with a ~5% probability of a second line being restored — the single-line fragility assumption holds in ~95% of scenarios.

**Tool audit accuracy:** Agent says "Fetch URL: Mixed - IAEA.org returned 403." Ground truth shows 5 fetch_url calls with 2 errors (40%). The agent's characterization is accurate. ✓

**Worldview research note:** The research entry c7190690 contains an internal inconsistency (describes Dniprovska as "Operational — sole main line" in a table but later says it was disconnected March 24). However, the forecasting agent correctly resolved this by relying on the direct IAEA update text and the Metaculus question background. No impact on the forecast.

**No hallucinated evidence, no resolution misalignment, no pre-publication event issues. Forecast is well-grounded and appropriately calibrated.**
